title: Peppermint and kaolin clay toothpaste
subtitle: A fluoride-free clay toothpaste with bicarbonate of soda and peppermint essential oil
excerpt: Kaolin clay is a mild, fine-particle abrasive that makes a good base for a fluoride-free toothpaste: it cleans without scratching enamel and gives a smooth, creamy texture when blended with coconut oil. Bicarbonate of soda adds a second mild abrasive and helps neutralise mouth acid. Peppermint essential oil gives the familiar clean, cooling taste. Yield is approximately 70 g.
sourceNotes: Kaolin clay as a dental abrasive: FDA RDA (relative dentin abrasivity) value for kaolin is low, making it safe for enamel. Bicarbonate of soda in toothpaste: well-documented as a safe mild abrasive and pH neutraliser. Peppermint EO in dental products: GRAS (generally recognised as safe) for oral use at low concentrations; the amount here per use (a pea-size portion) is negligible. Note: this formulation contains no fluoride. Dental health professionals recommend fluoride toothpaste as the primary dentifrice; this recipe is for supplementary or occasional use, not as a fluoride replacement. Not suitable for children under 3 years.

recipe.yieldDescription: Approximately 70 g (fills 1 small tin or tube)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale by multiplying all weights. Store in a sealed tin or jar; use a clean, dry spoon each time to avoid water contamination.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ready to use immediately. Store with a tight-fitting lid.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months if kept dry. Water contamination shortens shelf life considerably; use a clean dry spoon every time. Discard if the paste develops mould, an off smell, or separates into liquid and solid.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mild scratching agent used in toothpaste to remove surface stains and plaque. The relative dentin abrasivity (RDA) value measures how harsh an abrasive is; kaolin and bicarbonate of soda are both low-RDA abrasives safe for regular use without damaging enamel.

content.text: This toothpaste uses kaolin clay as its primary

: it is fine-particle, very mild, and gives a smooth, creamy texture when blended with coconut oil. Bicarbonate of soda adds a second mild abrasive and helps keep the mouth's pH neutral during brushing. Peppermint essential oil gives the expected clean, cooling mint flavour. This formulation contains no fluoride: dental health professionals recommend fluoride toothpaste as the primary dentifrice, so this recipe works well as an occasional alternative rather than a daily replacement. Do not give to children under 3 years. Do not swallow.

items.prepNote: Cosmetic or food-grade kaolin; fine white clay
Fine grade; mild abrasive and pH neutraliser
Melted if solid; forms the paste base
Keeps the paste smooth and prevents drying
Flavour and freshness; do not increase

content.text: If the coconut oil is solid, gently melt it in a heatproof bowl over warm water. Remove from the heat and allow to cool until no longer warm to the touch. Add the kaolin clay and bicarbonate of soda and stir to combine into a smooth paste. Add the vegetable glycerine and stir again. Finally, add the peppermint essential oil and mix thoroughly.
Spoon the paste into the salve tin. Smooth the top and press down firmly to remove air pockets. Seal with the lid and label with the batch date.

Use a clean, dry spoon or spatula to scoop a pea-size amount onto a dry toothbrush. Do not dip the wet toothbrush directly into the tin; this introduces water and shortens shelf life. Brush as normal for 2 minutes. Do not swallow. Rinse thoroughly.

Store in a cool, dry place with the lid closed between uses. The paste keeps 3 months if kept dry. Always use a clean, dry spoon; water contamination causes spoilage. Discard if the paste separates, grows mould, or develops an off smell. In summer, coconut oil in the paste may soften; this does not affect the function.
